summary:
Small clay or terracotta figurines — typically depicting human or animal forms — appear at Neolithic sites across the Levant and Anatolia from approximately 9000 BCE onward, predating the development of functional pottery by several thousand years in the same region. These figurines, made from fired or sun-dried clay, are among the earliest ceramics produced in the Near East and provide evidence that clay firing was initially applied to symbolic and ritual objects before it was adopted for practical containers.

The figurines from [[event:18|PPNB]] sites such as [[event:7|Ain Ghazal]] and other Levantine communities depict female forms (sometimes described as "Venus figurines" in older literature), animals, and occasionally male or indeterminate human figures. Their interpretation is debated — they may represent fertility symbols, ancestor figures, healing charms, or simply artistic objects. What is clear is that early Neolithic communities in the Levant had a rich tradition of symbolic and representational art in portable clay, contributing to a broader picture of ideological complexity in these early farming societies that also included [[event:1323|skull cult practices]], communal architectural investments, and participation in long-distance exchange networks for prestige materials.
